correctness.description: Does the solution pass all automated tests in tests/test_solution.py?
signals: GET /users/{id} returns 200 for an existing user
GET /users/{id} returns 404 (not a 500 crash) for a missing user
GET /users still returns the full list unaffected

edge_case_handling.description: Does the fix handle boundary and invalid inputs, not just the happy path?
signals: Non-existent user IDs return 404 rather than raising
The fix doesn't assume user_id is always a valid dict key

code_readability.description: Is the fix minimal, clear, and idiomatic?
signals: Uses HTTPException rather than manually constructing a response
No unrelated changes bundled into the fix

pattern_recognition.description: Does the user understand WHY dict[] access is dangerous here, not just that .get() 'fixes' it?
signals: Can explain that unhandled exceptions become 500s and can leak internals
Recognizes this as a general 'validate before use' pattern, not a one-off fix

what_senior_would_catch.description: One specific thing a real engineer would flag in review.
signals: A senior engineer would ask: what does the client see when this 500s in production, and does it leak a stack trace?
